title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
ctrl+space abre Intellisense. La sintaxis común es:

objeto.método
objeto.propiedad = valor



Lo que sigue a continuación es una subrutina
Sub mi_primera_subrutina()

    ' objeto.método
    Worksheets.Add

    ' objeto.propiedad = valor
    ' añadir valor a celda
    Range("A1").Value = "Erick Iván"
    Range("A2").Value = Date
    Range("A3").Value = Time
    
    ' formato a celda
    Range("A1:A3").Interior.Color = rgbDarkMagenta
    Range("A1:A3").Font.Color = rgbWhite
    
    ' Cambiar ancho de columna
    Columns("A").AutoFit

End Sub
Otros ejemplos de subrutinas. La siguiente asigna un color aleatorio a la celda seleccionada.
Sub color_aleatorio_celda_seleccionada()

    Selection.Interior.Color = WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 16777215)

End Sub
Esta subrutina quita el formato de la celda seleccionada.
Sub limpiar_formato_celda()

    Selection.ClearFormats
    
End Sub



VBA es un lenguaje de programación orientado a objetos y tiene 4 partes fundamentales:

Objeto
Colección
Método
Propiedad

Sub como_funciona_vba()
    
    Range("A1:B1").Select

    ' Aplicar método a objeto
    ' Objeto.Método

    Selection.Merge
    
    ' Cambiar propiedad de un objeto
    ' Objeto.Propiedad = Algo
    
    Range("A1").Value = "How VBA works"
    
    Range("A1").RowHeight = 20
    
    Range("A2").Value = "Item"
    Range("B2").Value = "Description"
    
    Range("A1").Interior.Color = rgbLightBlue
    Range("A1").Font.Size = 16
    
    Range("B2").EntireColumn.AutoFit
    
End Sub
Es posible combinar métodos y propiedades



Hay diferentes formas de definir los parámetros de un método. La siguiente subrutina sirve para imprimir (literalmente) parte de un libro de excel.
Sub imprimir()
    
    ThisWorkbook.PrintOut 1, 2, 5
    
    ' Para saltarse parámetros, escribe la coma nada más
    ThisWorkbook.PrintOut , , 5
    
    ' Si incluyo el nombre de los parámetros, debo nombrar todos
    ThisWorkbook.PrintOut Copies:=5, From:=1
    
    ' Para hacer linebreaks
    ThisWorkbook.PrintOut _
        From:=1, _
        To:=2, _
        Copies:=5

End Sub
Otro ejemplo. La siguiente subrutina copia una celda y pega únicamente el valor de la celda copiada a otra celda.
Sub pegar_valor()

    Range("A1").Copy
    
    Range("A3").PasteSpecial xlPasteValues

End Sub
Una subrutina más compleja en la que se ejemplifica la definición de parámetros.
Sub ejercicio_parametros()
    
    ' Añade hoja a la izquierda de la que está actualmente activa
    Worksheets.Add Before:=ActiveSheet
    
    ' Añadir borde a la celda actualmente activa
    ' xl constante simbólica para no tener que memorizar números
    ActiveCell.BorderAround xlDash, xlThin, , rgbRed
    
    ' Primero activamos una celda y luego le damos formato
    Range("A2").Activate
    
    ActiveCell.BorderAround _
        LineStyle:=xlDash, _
        Weight:=xlMedium, _
        Color:=rgbDarkCyan
    
    ' Sin embargo, no es necesario primero activar la celda
    Range("A4").BorderAround _
        LineStyle:=xlDashDot, _
        Weight:=xlThick, _
        Color:=rgbDarkMagenta
    
    ' Copiamos la celda activa y luego la pegamos con todo y formato a otra celda
    ActiveCell.Copy
    
    Range("B3").PasteSpecial _
        Paste:=xlPasteFormats
    

    
End Sub
Notemos que la última celda que activamos fue la A2 así que copiamos el formato de esa celda y la pegamos en al celda B3. No copiamos la última celda a la que le dimos formato, que en este caso fue la A4.




Sub using_parentheses()

    ' Métodos que no requieren paréntesis no regresan un resultado
    
    ThisWorkbook.PrintOut 1, 2, 5
    
    Worksheets.Add ActiveSheet, , 2
    
    ActiveCell.BorderAround xlDash, xlThin, , rgbRed
    
    ActiveSheet.Protect Password:="Secret", Contents:=True
    
    ' Si agregas paréntesis, obtienes un error de sintaxis
    
    ThisWorkbook.PrintOut (1, 2, 5)
    
    ' En estos dos casos, la opción sin paréntesis regresa un error de sintaxis

    Cells.Find "Hola".select
    
    Cells.Find("Hola").Select
    
    ' También aquí

    ActiveCell.Offset 1, 0.select
    
    ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select
    
    ' La siguiente línea no regresa un error de sintaxis
    ActiveSheet.Shapes("Oval l").Fill.ForeColor = rgbGreen
    
    ' Lo siguiente no hace lo que quiero hacer.
    ' Estamos referenciando la propiedad ChartType a ActiveSheet
    
    Charts.Add Before:=ActiveSheet.ChartType = xlPie
    
    ' Esto es lo que queremos
    
    Charts.Add(Before:=ActiveSheet).ChartType = xlPie
    
    ' Cambiar color interior. La opción sin paréntesis regresa un error
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = RGB(200, 0, 200)
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = RGB 200, 0, 200
End Sub
RGB() As Long. Long es un tipo de dato. Nos permite saber que debemos utilizar paréntesis. También, al poner paréntesis, aparece el Intellisense, lo cual significa que estamos haciendo bien al agregar paréntesis. Si algo no regresa nada, no aparece al final del Intelisense el “As Long”



Esta es una subrutina que colorea las celdas de los finalistas
Sub HighlightSemiFinalists()

    Range("A4").Select  'select Belgium
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    ActiveCell.Font.Color = rgbWhite
    ActiveCell.Font.Bold = True
    
    Range("A8").Select  'select Croatia
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    ActiveCell.Font.Color = rgbWhite
    ActiveCell.Font.Bold = True
    
    Range("A11").Select 'select England
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    ActiveCell.Font.Color = rgbWhite
    ActiveCell.Font.Bold = True
    
    Range("A12").Select 'select France
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    ActiveCell.Font.Color = rgbWhite
    ActiveCell.Font.Bold = True
    
End Sub
Hay mucho código repetido, así que podemos crear el siguiente procedimiento
Sub format_the_active_cell()

    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    ActiveCell.Font.Color = rgbWhite
    ActiveCell.Font.Bold = True

End Sub
Y sustituir las líneas de código repetidas con este procedimiento.
Sub HighlightSemiFinalists()

    Range("A4").Select  'select Belgium
    
    format_the_active_cell
    
    Range("A8").Select  'select Croatia
    
    format_the_active_cell
    
    Range("A11").Select 'select England
    
    format_the_active_cell
    
    Range("A12").Select 'select France
    
    ' También puedes poner 'Call' antes de la subrutina
    ' Hace más obvio que es una subrutina que nosotros creamos
    Call format_the_active_cell
    
End Sub
Notemos que podemos poner Call antes de la subrutina que estamos llamando para hacer explícito que fue creada por el usuario.
Por default, las subrutinas son públicas (a eso se le llama el Scope), pero podemos escribir antes de Sub la palabra Public. Las subrutinas públicas pueden ser llamadas desde otro módulo.
Public Sub format_the_active_cell()

    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    ActiveCell.Font.Color = rgbWhite
    ActiveCell.Font.Bold = True

End Sub
Si la hacemos privada, entonces solo podemos llamar este procedimiento desde el módulo en el que fue definido y no desde otro módulo.
Private Sub format_the_active_cell()

    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    ActiveCell.Font.Color = rgbWhite
    ActiveCell.Font.Bold = True

End Sub



Para ver el examinador de objetos:




Al presionar Ayuda se abre una ventana de Microsoft que explica el funcionamiento de este método

También es posible situando la barra vertical en el objeto del que quieras ayuda y presionando F1. De igual modo, se abre una ventana de Microsoft en el navegador.





En la siguiente subrutina repetimos mucho código
Sub not_using_with()

    Range("A1").Font.Bold = True
    Range("A1").Font.Size = 14
    Range("A1").Font.Color = rgbOrange
    Range("A1").Font.Name = "Arial"
    Range("A1").Font.Italic = True
    
End Sub
Con un bloque With nos podemos ahorrar mucho el código repetido. La indentación no es necesaria.
Sub using_with()

    With Range("A2").Font
        .Bold = True
        .Size = 14
        .Color = rgbOrange
        .Name = "Arial"
        .Italic = True
    End With

End Sub
Se obtiene el mismo resultado con ambas subrutinas.



with

En la siguiente subrutina repetimos mucho código
Sub not_using_with()

    Range("A1").Font.Size = 14

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
2.1 Seleccionar objetos

2.1.1 Seleccionar celdas
Esta es la forma más común de seleccionar celdas.
Sub seleccionar_por_referencia_de_celda()

    Range("A1").Select
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbLime

End Sub
Esta es otra forma menos utilizada
Sub seleccionar_por_referencia_de_celda_alt()

    [A2].Select
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbRed

End Sub
En lugar de la palabra Range se puede utiliza la palabra Cell.
Sub seleccionar_por_numero_de_columna_y_fila()
    
    ' No se abre el Intellisense después del punto
    Cells(3, 1).Select
    
    ActiveCell.Interior.Color = rgbPink
    
End Sub
Lo que se obtiene después de utilizar los tres métodos



seleccionar celdas


Podemos seleccionar varias celdas al mismo tiempo. Tenemos la siguiente tabla.

Primero le damos formato al encabezado con el siguiente código.
Sub seleccionar_bloque_celdas()
    
    ' seleccionar tres celdas
    Range("A1:D1").Select
    
    ' Aunque selecciones varias, solo una está activa
    ' Después del primer punto no se abre el Intellisense
    Selection.Interior.Color = rgbPaleGoldenrod
    Selection.Font.Bold = True

End Sub
Podemos seleccionar celdas aunque no estén adyacentes. Coloreamos de morado las filas de las personas que tienen a un cuyo como mascota.
Sub seleccionar_celdas_no_adyacentes()

    Range("A3:D3, A5:D5").Select
    
    Selection.Interior.Color = rgbBlueViolet
    Selection.Font.Color = rgbWhite

End Sub
También podemos darle formato a los números de una selección. Vamos a darle formato a la columna Salario.
Sub formato_numero()

    Range("D2:D5").Select
    
    Selection.NumberFormat = "\$#,##0"

End Sub
Este es el resultado después de aplicar las tres subrutinas.



seleccionar celdas 3




2.1.2 Seleccionar hojas
Tenemos un libro con cuatro hojas.

En cada hoja hay una tabla similar. Queremos cambiar el nombre del encabezado de cada una de las tablas.
La hoja Paris está activa actualmente, así que no es necesario seleccionarla. Podemos cambiar el encabezado con el siguiente código.
Sub la_hoja_activa()

    Range("B2").Select
    
    ActiveCell.Value = ActiveSheet.Name

End Sub

Ahora queremos hacer lo mismo con la tabla en la hoja London
Code name (Name) nombre de código. Una forma es utilizar el nombre de la hoja con la palabra clave Worksheets.
Sub usar_nombre_hoja()

    Worksheets("London").Select
    
    Range("B2").Select
    
    ActiveCell.Value = ActiveSheet.Name

End Sub
Otra forma de hacerlo es utilizar el índice de la hoja. La hoja Milan es la número 3.
Sub usar_numero_hoja()

    Worksheets(3).Select
    
    Range("B2").Select
    
    ActiveCell.Value = ActiveSheet.Name

End Sub
Finalmente, podemos utilizar el nombre de código de la hoja. Es más conveniente hacerlo con este método pues es más probable que el nombre o índice de la hoja cambien.

En este cambiamos el nombre de código de la hoja Hull a HullSheet. Después cambiamos el encabezado de la tabla de la hoja Hull con el siguiente código.
Sub usar_nombre_codigo()

    HullSheet.Select
    
    Range("B2").Select
    
    ActiveCell.Value = ActiveSheet.Name

End Sub


2.1.3 Seleccionar libros
Tenemos cuatros libros

Q1 Total.xlsm
Jan Sales.xlsx
Feb Sales.xlsx
Mar Sales.xlsx

En el libro Q1 Total.xlsm tenemos la hoja Total, en la cual está la siguiente tabla

Para cada mes queremos obtener las ventas totales. Las ventas totales de Enero están el el libro Jan Sales.xlsx, en la hoja Total, en la celda B7.

Queremos copiar este valor y pegarlo a la celda B2 de la hoja Total del libro Q1 Total.xlsm. Lo hacemos con el siguiente código.
Sub copiar_ventas_enero()

    ' Primero activamos el libro Jan Sales.xlsx
    Workbooks("Jan Sales.xlsx").Activate
    
    ' Seleccionamos la hoja y celda que nos interesan
    Worksheets("Total").Select
    Range("B7").Select
    ActiveCell.Copy
    
    ' Regresamos al libro en donde queremos pegar el valor
    Workbooks("Q1 Total.xlsm").Activate
    
    ' Es buena práctica primero seleccionar la hoja
    ' Nos lo vamos a saltar, pues solo hay una hoja
    Range("B3").Select
    ActiveCell.PasteSpecial xlPasteValues

End Sub
Este es el resultado.

Podemos utilizar la expresión ThisWorkbook para referirnos al libro en el que se guardo el módulo. Es más conveniente pues podríamos cambiar el nombre del libro, en cuyo caso el código de arriba no funcionaría.
Sub copiar_ventas_febrero()

    Workbooks("Feb Sales.xlsx").Activate
    
    Worksheets("Total").Select
    Range("B7").Select
    ActiveCell.Copy
    
    ' Activamos el libro en el que el módulo está guardado
    ' El cual es el libro Q1 Total.xlsm
    ThisWorkbook.Activate
    
    Range("B4").Select
    ActiveCell.PasteSpecial xlPasteValues

End Sub



2.1.4 Referenciar
No es necesario utilizar el método Select para editar una celda, como se puede ver en el siguiente código, el cual, además, es más eficiente.
Sub copiar_ventas_marzo()

    ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Total").Range("B5").Value = _
        Workbooks("Mar Sales.xlsx").Worksheets("Total").Range("B7").Value

End Sub

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
2.2 Rangos

2.2.1 Propiedad End

Hace lo mismo que Ctrl + flecha
Ir hasta abajo de una tabla.
Sub ir_hasta_abajo_tabla()

    Range("A4").Select
    
    ' End no hace nada por sí solo
    ' Tenemos que aplicarle un método, en este caso Select
    ActiveCell.End(xlDown).Select

End Sub
Ir a la tabla que está debajo
Sub ir_siguiente_tabla_abajo()

    Range("A4").Select
    
    ActiveCell.End(xlDown).Select
    
    ActiveCell.End(xlDown).Select

End Sub
Ir a la última celda con datos (dos formas).
Sub ir_ultima_celda_con_datos()

    ' Última fila depende de la versión de Excel
    Range("A1046576").End(xlUp).Select

End Sub
Sub ir_ultima_celda_con_datos2()

    Cells(ActiveSheet.Rows.Count, 1).End(xlUp).Select

End Sub
Ir a la esquina inferior derecha.
Sub ir_esquina_inferior_derecha()

    Range("A4").Select
    
    ActiveCell.End(xlDown).End(xlToRight).Select

End Sub


2.2.2 Propiedad CurrentRegion
Si conocemos el rango de una tabla hay dos formas de seleccionarla. Supongamos que nos interesa aplicarle un método a la tabla AFC East (A5:D9). Podemos utilizar alguna de las dos sintaxis:

Range("A5:D9")
Range("A5","D9")

La propiedad CurrentRegion hace lo mismo que presionar Ctrl+E (Ctrl+A en Excel Gringo). Supongamos que queremos copiar la tabla AFC North (A12:D16) y pegarla en una hoja llamada AFC North. Eso lo logramos con la siguiente subrutina.
Sub copiar_afc_north()

    Range("A12").CurrentRegion.Copy _
        Destination:=Worksheets("AFC North").Range("A1")

End Sub
Otra forma un poco menos elegante para pegar la tabla NFC North (F12:I16) en una hoja llamada NFC North es la siguiente.
Sub copiar_nfc_north()
    Range("F12", Range("F12").End(xlDown).End(xlToRight)).Copy _
        Destination:=Worksheets("NFC North").Range("A1")
        
End Sub
Con Range("F12").End(xlDown).End(xlToRight) nos movemos hasta la esquina inferior derecha de la tabla.


2.2.3 Propiedad Offset
Para moverte hacia otra celda desde la celda activa. Sintaxis: Range.Offset(n,m).

n positiva: hacia abajo.
n negativa: hacia arriba.
m positiva: hacia la derecha.
m negativa: hacia la izquierda.


Queremos asignar a cada flecha un subrutina adecuada para mover la celda una posición en cualquier dirección.
Sub Go_To_A1()

    Range("A1").Select
    
End Sub

Sub move_down()

    ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select

End Sub

Sub move_up()

    ActiveCell.Offset(-1, 0).Select
    
End Sub

Sub move_right()

    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Select

End Sub

Sub move_left()

    ActiveCell.Offset(0, -1).Select

End Sub

Sub move_down_right()

    ActiveCell.Offset(1, 1).Select

End Sub

Sub move_up_right()

    ActiveCell.Offset(-1, 1).Select

End Sub

Sub move_down_left()

    ActiveCell.Offset(1, -1).Select

End Sub

Sub move_up_left()

    ActiveCell.Offset(-1, -1).Select

End Sub
Si tratas salir del libro, se genera un error.


2.2.4 Práctica: agregar fila a tabla
Escribimos una review y después queremos agregarla a la lista

Sub llenar_fila()

    Range("D1").End(xlDown).Offset(1, 0).Select
    
    ActiveCell.Value = Range("B2").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Value = Range("B3").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value = Range("B4").Value
    
    ' Cuidado con empezar con una lista vacía
    ' Error en tiempo de ejecución
    ' Nos movimos hasta la última fila y luego quisimos bajar una fila más
End Sub

Cuidado con empezar con una lista vacía, pues obtenemos un error en tiempo de ejecución. Esto ocurre pues nos movimos hasta la última fila y luego quisimos bajar una fila más. Para evitarlo podemos utilizar el siguiente código.
Sub llenar_fila_alt()
    
    ' Nos movemos hasta la última fila del libro
    ' Luego subimos hasta la última fila de la tabla
    ' Luego nos movemos una fila hacia abajo
    Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(1, 0).Select
    
    ActiveCell.Value = Range("B2").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Value = Range("B3").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value = Range("B4").Value
    
End Sub
Hay otra forma de hacer lo mismo sin tener que seleccionar ninguna celda, con lo que logramos que el código sea más eficiente.
Sub Add_To_List_Alternative()

Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(1, 0).Value = _
    Range("B2").Value

Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(0, 1).Value = _
    Range("B3").Value

Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(0, 2).Value = _
    Range("B4").Value

Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(0, 3).Value = _
    Range("B5").Value

End Sub

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
3.1 Instrucción If
Si no agregamos el título de la película, cuando queramos agregar otro registro las cosas no funcionarían como querríamos.

Para evitar esto podemos utilizar la instrucción If.
Sub Add_To_List()
        
    If Range("B2").Value = "" Then Exit Sub
        
    'Select the next blank cell in column D
    Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(1, 0).Select
    
    'Copy the entered values into the table
    ActiveCell.Value = Range("B2").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Value = Range("B3").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value = Range("B4").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = Range("B5").Value
    
End Sub
Así, cuando presionamos el botón, la subrutina terminará si la no agregamos el nombre de la película.

3.1.1 Bloque If
Sub Add_To_List()
        
    If Range("B2").Value = "" Then
        
        Range("B2").Interior.Color = rgbRed
        Range("B2").Select
        ActiveCell.Value = "Agrega película"
        
        Exit Sub
    
    End If
    
    ' Quitar color a celda de película
    Range("B2").Interior.Color = xlNone
    
    ' Agregar a la tabla
    agregar
    
End Sub



3.1.2 Else

Sub Roll_Again()

    Range("B2").Value = WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(1, 100)
    Range("D2").Value = WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(1, 100)
    Range("B2, D2").Interior.Color = rgbGray
    
End Sub
Sub pintar_ganador()

    Roll_Again
   
    If Range("B2").Value > Range("D2").Value Then
    
        Range("B2").Interior.Color = rgbGreen
        Range("D2").Interior.Color = rgbRed
    
    Else
    
        Range("B2").Interior.Color = rgbRed
        Range("D2").Interior.Color = rgbGreen
    
    End If
    
End Sub



3.1.3 ElseIf
Sub pintar_ganador()

    Roll_Again
   
    If Range("B2").Value > Range("D2").Value Then
    
        Range("B2").Interior.Color = rgbGreen
        Range("D2").Interior.Color = rgbRed
    
    ElseIf Range("B2").Value < Range("D2").Value Then
    
        Range("B2").Interior.Color = rgbRed
        Range("D2").Interior.Color = rgbGreen
        
    Else
        
        Range("B2, D2").Interior.Color = rgbOrange
        Range("C4").Value = "Empate"
    
    End If
    
End Sub



3.1.4 If anidados
Cambiamos el rango de los números aleatorios y el número grande más grande que podemos obtener es 6.
Sub pintar_ganador_2()

    Range("B2").Value = WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(1, 6)
    Range("D2").Value = WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(1, 6)
    Range("B3").Clear
    Range("D3").Clear
    Range("C4").Clear
    
    If Range("B2").Value > Range("D2").Value Then
    
        Range("B2").Interior.Color = rgbGreen
        Range("D2").Interior.Color = rgbRed
        
        Range("B3").Value = "Ganador"
        Range("D3").Value = "Perdedor"
        
        If Range("B2").Value = 6 Then
        
            Range("B3").Value = "TOP"
            Range("B3").Interior.Color = rgbGold
        
        End If
    
    ElseIf Range("B2").Value < Range("D2").Value Then
    
        Range("B2").Interior.Color = rgbRed
        Range("D2").Interior.Color = rgbGreen
        
        Range("B3").Value = "Perdedor"
        Range("D3").Value = "Ganador"
        
        If Range("D2").Value = 6 Then
            
            Range("D3").Value = "TOP"
            Range("D3").Interior.Color = rgbGold
            
        End If
        
    Else
        
        Range("B2, D2").Interior.Color = rgbOrange
        Range("C4").Value = "Empate"
        Range("C4").Interior.Color = rgbOrange
    
    End If
End Sub

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
3.2 Operadores lógicos

3.2.1 Or
Sub Add_To_List()
    
    Range("A5").ClearContents
    
    If Range("B2").Value = "" _
    Or Range("B3").Value = "" _
    Or Range("B4").Value = "" Then
        
        Range("A5").Value = "Missing Values"
        Exit Sub
        
    End If
    
    'Select the next blank cell in column D
    Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(1, 0).Select
    
    'Copy the entered values into the table
    ActiveCell.Value = Range("B2").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Value = Range("B3").Value
    ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value = Range("B4").Value
    
End Sub



3.2.2 And
Dependiendo de la calificación que le demos a la película, queremos pegar la información en alguna de las hojas que tiene el libro.

Sub Add_To_List()
    
    'Copy the entered data
    Range("B2:B4").Copy
    
    If Range("B4").Value <= 5 Then
    
        Worksheets("Rubbish").Select
    
    ElseIf Range("B4") > 5 And Range("B4") <= 8 Then
        
        Worksheets("OK").Select
        
    Else
    
        Worksheets("Great").Select
    
    End If
    
    Range("A1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(1, 0).PasteSpecial _
        Transpose:=True
    
End Sub

Utilizamos el argumento Transpose del método PasteSpecial.


3.2.3 Not

Sub agregar_lista()

    If Not IsDate(Range("B3").Value) Then
    
        Range("A5").Value = "Enter a valid date"
        Exit Sub
    End If
    
    Add_To_List
    
End Sub

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
3.3 Select Case
Sub Add_To_List()
    
    'Copy the entered data
    Range("B2:B4").Copy
    
    'Paste the copied cells
    Range("D1048576").End(xlUp).Offset(1, 0).Select
    ActiveCell.PasteSpecial Transpose:=True
    
    Select Case Range("B4").Value
        Case 0
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = "Horrible"
        Case 1, 2, 3
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = "Mala"
        Case 4 To 6
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = "Maso"
        Case 6 To 8
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = "Buena"
        Case Is > 8
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = "Excelente"
        Case Else
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = "No reconocido"
    End Select
    
End Sub

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
3.4 Funciones condicionales

3.4.1 IIf

Sub Predict_Score()

    Range("B3").Value = _
        WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
        
    Range("C3").Value = _
        WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
    
    Range("C7").Value = IIf( _
        Range("B3").Value > Range("C3").Value, _
        Range("B2").Value, _
        IIf( _
            Range("B3").Value < Range("C3").Value, _
            Range("C2").Value, _
            "Draw"))

End Sub


3.4.2 Switch

Sub WhatsMyBMI()
    
    'calculate BMI = WeightKg / (HeightM * HeightM)
    Range("B4").Value = _
        Range("B3").Value / (Range("B2").Value * Range("B2").Value)
    
    Range("B5").Value = Switch( _
        Range("B4").Value < 18.5, "Underweight", _
        Range("B4").Value < 25, "Healthy", _
        Range("B4").Value < 30, "Overweight", _
        Range("B4").Value < 40, "Obese", _
        True, "Morbidly Obese")
    ' True en lugar de Range("B4").Value >= 40
    
    Columns("B").AutoFit
End Sub

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
3.5 Bucles

3.5.1 Do Loop
Tenemos 8 hojas correspondientes a los grupos desde el A hasta el H. Queremos obtener resultados aleatorios de cada partido de cada uno de los grupos.

Sub Predict_Scores()

    'Select first worksheet
    Worksheets(1).Select
    
    Do
        'Select first match date
        Range("A3").Select
        
        Do
            'Generate random scores
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value = _
                WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
                
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 4).Value = _
                WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
            
            ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select
            
            If ActiveCell.Value = "" Then Exit Do
            
        Loop
        ' No sabe que hay una última hoja
        If ActiveSheet.Next Is Nothing Then Exit Do
        
        ActiveSheet.Next.Select
    
    Loop
    
End Sub


3.5.2 Do Until Loop
Nos evita tener que utilizar un If para salir del bucle.
Si la condición es verdadera, entonces el código ya no se ejecuta:
Do Until ' condición

    ' código
    
Loop
Si la condición es verdadera, el bucle termina, pero antes de eso el código se ejecutó al menos una vez:
Do 

    ' código
    
Loop Until ' condición
Con el siguiente código hacemos lo mismo que en la sección anterior. Notemos que en el bucle que está anidado escribimos Until después de Do para terminar el bucle una vez que la celda activa esté vacía.
Sub Predict_Scores2()

    Worksheets(1).Select
    
    Do
    
        Range("A3").Select
        
        Do Until ActiveCell.Value = ""
            
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value = _
                WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
                
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 4).Value = _
                WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
            
            ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select
            
            ' If ActiveCell.Value = "" Then Exit Do
            
        Loop
        
        ' Salir del Loop de este modo para evitar errores
        If ActiveSheet.Next Is Nothing Then Exit Do
        
        ActiveSheet.Next.Select
    
    Loop
    
End Sub
En este caso, poner el Until después del Loop daría el mismo resultado.
Notemos que para salir del bucle principal sí es necesario hacerlo con una instrucción If.
Si utilizamos el Until después del Do, entonces no se generarían resultados del grupo H. Antes de que termina el bucle seleccionamos la hoja siguiente a la actual. Al final seleccionaríamos la hoja del grupo H, luego el Until evaluaría que no hay siguiente hoja, por lo que el código terminaría antes de llenar los resultados del grupo H. Pasa lo mismo si ponemos el Until después del Loop.


3.5.3 Do While Loop
Cambiamos Until ActiveCell.Value = "" por While ActiveCell.Value <> ""
Sub Predict_Scores3()

    Worksheets(1).Select
    
    Do
    
        Range("A3").Select
        
        Do While ActiveCell.Value <> ""
            
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value = _
                WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
                
            ActiveCell.Offset(0, 4).Value = _
                WorksheetFunction.RandBetween(0, 5)
            
            ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select
            
        Loop
        
        If ActiveSheet.Next Is Nothing Then Exit Do
        
        ActiveSheet.Next.Select
    
    Loop
    
End Sub

title: VBA por WiseOwl
text:
4.1 Variables básicas

4.1.1 Dim
Es buena práctica definir variables para hacer el código más fácil de leer.
Sub ProcessBMIList()
    
    ' Dimension (Fortran) Declare in memory
    Dim WeightKg As Double
    Dim HeightM As Double
    Dim BMI As Double
    Dim BMIBand As String

    Range("A3").Select
    
    Do Until ActiveCell.Value = ""
        
        WeightKg = ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Value
        HeightM = ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value
        
        BMI = WeightKg / (HeightM ^ 2)
        ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = BMI
        
        BMIBand = Switch( _
            BMI < 18.5, "Underweight", _
            BMI < 25, "Healthy", _
            BMI < 30, "Overweight", _
            True, "Obese")
        ActiveCell.Offset(0, 4).Value = BMIBand

        ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select
        
    Loop
    
End Sub



4.1.2 Debugging
Solo cuando corremos la subrutina paso a paso.

Ventana Locales


Ventana inspección






4.1.3 Variables no declaradas
No es necesario utilizar la instrucción Dim para declarar variables. El código siguiente realiza la misma función.
Sub ProcessBMIList()

'    Dim WeightKg As Double
'    Dim HeightM As Double
'    Dim BMI As Double
'    Dim BMIBand As String

    Range("A3").Select
    
    Do Until ActiveCell.Value = ""
        
        WeightKg = ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Value
        HeightM = ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value
        
        BMI = WeightKg / (HeightM * HeightM)
        
        BMIBand = Switch( _
            BMI < 18.5, "Underweight", _
            BMI < 25, "Healthy weight", _
            BMI < 30, "Overweight", _
            BMI >= 30, "Obese")
        
        ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = BMI
        ActiveCell.Offset(0, 4).Value = BMIBand
        
        ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select
    Loop
    
End Sub
Sin embargo, el tipo de las variables ahora es Variant, lo cual puede guardar cualquier tipo de dato, desde números hasta strings.

Empty es el subtipo de datos. Una vez que se guarde algo en la variable, su subtipo cambiará. En el caso de WeightKg, el subtipo cambiaría a Double. El tipo Double tiene un tamaño de almacenamiento de 8 bytes, mientras que el tipo Variant pesa 16 bytes, el doble.
El siguiente es un ejemplo de por qué es buena práctica declarar las variables.

Para que sea obligatorio declarar todas las variables de un módulo, hay que escribir la instrucción Option Explicit al principio.
Option Explicit

Sub ProcessBMIList()

...
Al correr el código, aparece esta ventana de error y además el editor señala qué variable no se declaró.

Para evitar que escribir mal una variable rompa nuestro código, se recomienda ir a Herramienta>Opciones y marcar la casilla “Requerir declaración de variables”. De esta manera, cada ves que se crea un nuevo módulo, al principio de éste aparecerá automáticamente la instrucción Option Explicit.



4.1.4 Variables a nivel de módulo
Option Explicit

Sub ProcessBMIList()

    Dim WeightKg As Double
    Dim HeightM As Double
    Dim BMI As Double
    Dim BMIBand As String

    Range("A3").Select
    
    Do Until ActiveCell.Value = ""
        
        WeightKg = ActiveCell.Offset(0, 1).Value
        HeightM = ActiveCell.Offset(0, 2).Value
        
        CalculateBMI
        
        BMIBand = Switch( _
            BMI < 18.5, "Underweight", _
            BMI < 25, "Healthy weight", _
            BMI < 30, "Overweight", _
            BMI >= 30, "Obese")
        
        ActiveCell.Offset(0, 3).Value = BMI
        ActiveCell.Offset(0, 4).Value = BMIBand
        
        ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Select
    Loop
    
End Sub

Sub CalculateBMI()

    BMI = WeightKg / (HeightM * HeightM)

End Sub

Aparece este error pues la variable WeightKg no ha sido definida. En realidad, la subrutina CalculateBMI no tiene acceso a la variable WeightKg que fue declarada en la subrutina ProcessBMIList. Se deben declarar las variables al principio del módulo para evitar este error.
Option Explicit

Dim WeightKg As Double
Dim HeightM As Double
Dim BMI As Double

Sub ProcessBMIList()

    Dim BMIBand As String
    ' Resto del código

End Sub

Sub CalculateBMI()

    BMI = WeightKg / (HeightM * HeightM)

End Sub
Si corremos el código paso a paso, vemos que en la ventana Locales solo aparece la variable BMIBand.

Pero si le damos click a la casilla con el signo de más a la izquierda de Module1, aparecen todas las variables declaradas a nivel módulo.

Notemos que las otras variables ya tienen un valor. Esto se debe a que las variables definidas a nivel módulo retienen su valor aun después de que la subrutina que las referenció terminó. Hay que tener cuidado con eso.
En lugar de Dim podemos utilizar la instrucción Private. Sin embargo, el comportamiento del módulo se mantiene igual. Solo es para hacer incluso todavía más claro que son variables a nivel módulo y no a nivel local, como BMIBand.
Option Explicit

Private WeightKg As Double
Private HeightM As Double
Private BMI As Double

...


4.1.5 Variables públicas

title: Introduction to Regression with statsmodels in Python
text:
Linear regression and logistic regression are two of the most widely used statistical models. They act like master keys, unlocking the secrets hidden in your data. In this course, you’ll gain the skills you need to fit simple linear and logistic regressions. Through hands-on exercises, you’ll explore the relationships between variables in real-world datasets, including motor insurance claims, Taiwan house prices, fish sizes, and more. By the end of this course, you’ll know how to make predictions from your data, quantify model performance, and diagnose problems with model fit.
What you’ll learn: - Assess the accuracy and limitations of model predictions, including the effects of extrapolation and variable transformation - Define the roles of coefficients, residuals, R-squared, residual standard error, leverage, and Cook’s distance within regression output - Differentiate between probability, odds ratio, log-odds, and most-likely outcome when interpreting logistic regression results and confusion matrices - Evaluate model fit by interpreting numerical metrics and diagnostic plots for both linear and logistic regression - Identify appropriate scenarios for applying simple linear and logistic regression with statsmodels in Python
import pandas as pd

title: Introduction to Regression with statsmodels in Python
text:
1 Simple Linear Regression Modeling
You’ll learn the basics of this popular statistical model, what regression is, and how linear and logistic regressions differ. You’ll then learn how to fit simple linear regression models with numeric and categorical explanatory variables, and how to describe the relationship between the response and explanatory variables using model coefficients.

1.1 A tale of two variables
This dataset on Swedish motor insurance claims is as simple as it gets. Each row represents a region in Sweden, and the two variables are the number of claims made in that region, and the total payment made by the insurance company for those claims, in Swedish krona.

This course assumes you have experience with calculating descriptive statistics on variables in a DataFrame. For example, calculating the mean of each variable. We can use pandas for this, as shown here. The course also assumes you understand the correlation between two variables. Here, the correlation is 0.91, a strong positive correlation. That means that as the number of claims increases, the total payment typically increases as well.

Regression models are a class of statistical models that let you explore the relationship between a response variable and some explanatory variables. That is, given some explanatory variables, you can make predictions about the value of the response variable. In the insurance dataset, if you know the number of claims made in a region, you can predict the amount that the insurance company has to pay out. That lets you do thought experiments like asking how much the company would need to pay if the number of claims increased to 200.

The response variable, the one you want to make predictions on, is also known as the dependent variable or the y variable. These terms are completely interchangeable. Explanatory variables, used to explain how the predictions will change, are also known as independent variables or x variables. Again, these terms are interchangeable.
In this course we’re going to look at two types of regression. Linear regression is used when the response variable is numeric, like in the motor insurance dataset. Logistic regression is used when the response variable is logical. That is, it takes True or False values. We’ll limit the scope further to only consider simple linear regression and simple logistic regression. This means you only have a single explanatory variable.
Before you start running regression models, it’s a good idea to visualize your dataset. To visualize the relationship between two numeric variables, you can use a scatter plot. The course assumes that your data visualization skills are strong enough that you can understand the seaborn code written here. On the plot, you can see that the total payment increases as the number of claims increases. It would be nice to be able to describe this increase more precisely.

One refinement we can make is to add a trend line to the scatter plot. A trend line means fitting a line that follows the data points. In seaborn, trend lines are drawn using the regplot() function, which adds a trend line calculated using linear regression. By default, regplot() adds a confidence interval around the line, which we can remove by setting the ci argument to None. The trend line is mostly quite close to the data points, so we can say that the linear regression is a reasonable fit.

Here’s the plan for the course.

We’ll visualize and fit linear regressions.
Then we’ll make predictions with them.
Ee’ll look at ways of quantifying whether or not the model is a good fit.
In the final chapter, we’ll run through this flow again using logistic regression models.

Before we dive into the first exercise, a word on Python packages for regression. Both statsmodels and scikit-learn can be used. However, statsmodels is more optimized for insight, whereas scikit-learn is more optimized for prediction. Since we’ll focus on insight, we’ll be using statsmodels in this course
Which one is the response variable?
Regression lets you predict the values of a response variable from known values of explanatory variables. Which variable you use as the response variable depends on the question you are trying to answer, but in many datasets, there will be an obvious choice for variables that would be interesting to predict. Over the next few exercises, you’ll explore a Taiwan real estate dataset with four variables.







Variable
Meaning




dist_to_mrt_station_m
Distance to nearest MRT metro station, in meters.


n_convenience
No. of convenience stores in walking distance.


house_age_years
The age of the house, in years, in three groups.

414 rows × 4 columns



Predicting prices is a common business task, so house price makes a good response variable.
Visualizing two numeric variables
Before you can run any statistical models, it’s usually a good idea to visualize your dataset. Here, you’ll look at the relationship between house price per area and the number of nearby convenience stores using the Taiwan real estate dataset.
One challenge in this dataset is that the number of convenience stores contains integer data, causing points to overlap. To solve this, you will make the points transparent.
Instructions: - Using taiwan_real_estate, draw a scatter plot of "price_twd_msq" (y-axis) versus "n_convenience" (x-axis).

sns.scatterplot(x="n_convenience",
                y="price_twd_msq",
                data=taiwan_real_estate)

plt.show()









Draw a trend line calculated using linear regression. Omit the confidence interval ribbon. Note: The scatter_kws argument makes the data points 50% transparent.


# Draw a trend line on the scatter plot of price_twd_msq vs. n_convenience 
sns.regplot(x="n_convenience",
            y="price_twd_msq",
            data=taiwan_real_estate,
            ci=None,
            scatter_kws={'alpha': 0.5})

plt.show()








Scatter plots are the standard way to visualize the relationship between two numeric variables, and seaborn makes adding linear trend lines easy.


1.2 Fitting a linear regression
You may have noticed that the linear regression trend lines in the scatter plots were straight lines. That’s a defining feature of a linear regression. Straight lines are completely defined by two properties. The intercept is the \(y\) value when \(x\) is zero. The slope is the steepness of the line, equal to the amount \(y\) increases if you increase \(x\) by one. The equation for a straight line is that the y value is
\[y = \text{intercept} + \text{slope} \cdot x\]
Here’s the trend line from the Swedish insurance dataset.

Let’s try to estimate the intercept. To find the intercept, look at where the trend line intersects the \(y\) axis. It’s less than half way to the 50 mark, so I’d guess it’s about 20.

To estimate the slope, we need two points. To make the guessing easier, I’ve chosen points where the line is close to the gridlines.

First, we calculate the change in \(y\) values between the points. One \(y\) value is about 400 and the other is about one 150, so the difference is 250.

Now we do the same for the \(x\) axis. One point is at one 110, the other at 40. So the difference is 70.

To estimate the slope we divide one number by the other. 250 divided by 70 is about 3.5, so that is our estimate for the slope.

Let’s run a linear regression to check our guess. To run a linear regression model, you import the ols() function from statsmodels.formula.api. OLS stands for ordinary least squares, which is a type of regression, and is commonly used. The function ols() takes two arguments. The first argument is a formula: the response variable is written to the left of the tilde, and the explanatory variable is written to the right. The data argument takes the DataFrame containing the variables. To actually fit the model, you add the .fit() method to your freshly created model object. When you print the resulting model, it’s helpful to use the .params attribute, which contains the model’s parameters. This will result in two coefficients. These coefficients are the intercept and slope of the straight line. It seems our guesses were pretty close. The intercept is very close to our estimate of 20. The slope, indicated here as n_claims, is 3.4, slightly lower than what we guessed.

That means that we expect the total payment to be 20 plus 3.4 times the number of claims.
\[\verb|total_payment_sek| = 19.99 + 3.41 \cdot \verb|n_claims|\]
So for every additional claim, we expect the total payment to increase by 3.4.
Linear regression with ols()
While sns.regplot() can display a linear regression trend line, it doesn’t give you access to the intercept and slope as variables, or allow you to work with the model results as variables. That means that sometimes you’ll need to run a linear regression yourself.
Time to run your first model! TWD is an abbreviation for Taiwan dollars.
Instructions:

Import the ols() function from the statsmodels.formula.api package.

from statsmodels.formula.api import ols

Run a linear regression with price_twd_msq as the response variable, n_convenience as the explanatory variable, and taiwan_real_estate as the dataset. Name it mdl_price_vs_conv.
Fit the model.
Print the parameters of the fitted model.


# Create the model object
mdl_price_vs_conv = ols("price_twd_msq ~ n_convenience", data=taiwan_real_estate)

# Fit the model
mdl_price_vs_conv = mdl_price_vs_conv.fit()

# Print the parameters of the fitted model
print(mdl_price_vs_conv.params)

Intercept       8.22
n_convenience   0.80
dtype: float64



The model had an Intercept coefficient of 8.2242. What does this mean? On average, a house with zero convenience stores nearby had a price of 8.2242 TWD per square meter.
The model had an n_convenience coefficient of 0.7981. What does this mean? If you increase the number of nearby convenience stores by one, then the expected increase in house price is 0.7981 TWD per square meter.

The intercept is positive, so a house with no convenience stores nearby still has a positive price. The coefficient for convenience stores is also positive, so as the number of nearby convenience stores increases, so does the price of the house.


1.3 Categorical explanatory variables
So far we looked at running a linear regression using a numeric explanatory variable. Now let’s look at what happens with a categorical explanatory variable. Let’s take a look at some data on the masses of fish sold at a fish market. Each row of data contains the species of a fish, and its mass. The mass will be the response variable.

To visualize the data, scatter plots aren’t ideal because species is categorical. Instead, we can draw a histogram for each of the species. To give a separate panel to each species, I use seaborn’s displot() function. This takes a DataFrame as the data argument, the variable of interest as x, and the variable you want to split on as col. It also takes an optional col_wrap argument to specify the number of plots per row. Because the dataset is fairly small, I also set the bins argument to 9. By default, displot() creates histograms.

Let’s calculate some summary statistics. First we group by species, then we calculate their mean masses. You can see that the mean mass of a bream is 618 grams. The mean mass for a perch is 382 grams, and so on.

Let’s run a linear regression using mass as the response variable and species as the explanatory variable. The syntax is the same: you call ols(), passing a formula with the response variable on the left and the explanatory variable on the right, and setting the data argument to the DataFrame. We fit the model using the .fit() method, and retrieve the parameters using .params on the fitted model. This time we have four coefficients: an intercept, and one for three of the fish species. A coefficient for bream is missing, but the number for the intercept looks familiar. The intercept is the mean mass of the bream that you just calculated. You might wonder what the other coefficients are, and why perch has a negative coefficient, since fish masses can’t be negative.

The coefficients for each category are calculated relative to the intercept. This way of displaying results can be useful for models with multiple explanatory variables, but for simple linear regression, it’s just confusing. Fortunately, we can fix it. By changing the formula slightly to append + 0, we specify that all the coefficients should be given relative to zero. Equivalently, it means we are fitting a linear regression without an intercept term. If you subtract 235.59 from 617.83, you get 382.24, which is the mean mass of a perch. Now these coefficients make more sense. They are all just the mean masses for each species. This is a reassuringly boring result. When you only have a single, categorical explanatory variable, the linear regression coefficients are simply the means of each category.

Visualizing numeric vs. categorical
If the explanatory variable is categorical, the scatter plot that you used before to visualize the data doesn’t make sense. Instead, a good option is to draw a histogram for each category.
The Taiwan real estate dataset has a categorical variable in the form of the age of each house. The ages have been split into 3 groups: 0 to 15 years, 15 to 30 years, and 30 to 45 years.
Instructions:

Using taiwan_real_estate, plot a histogram of price_twd_msq with 10 bins. Split the plot by house_age_years to give 3 panels.


sns.displot(data=taiwan_real_estate,
            x="price_twd_msq",
            col="house_age_years",
            bins=10)

plt.show()








It appears that new houses are the most expensive on average, and the medium aged ones (15 to 30 years) are the cheapest.
Calculating means by category
A good way to explore categorical variables further is to calculate summary statistics for each category. For example, you can calculate the mean and median of your response variable, grouped by a categorical variable. As such, you can compare each category in more detail.
Here, you’ll look at grouped means for the house prices in the Taiwan real estate dataset. This will help you understand the output of a linear regression with a categorical variable.
Instructions:

Group taiwan_real_estate by house_age_years and calculate the mean price (price_twd_msq) for each age group. Assign the result to mean_price_by_age.
Print the result and inspect the output.


# Calculate the mean of price_twd_msq, grouped by house age
mean_price_by_age = taiwan_real_estate.groupby("house_age_years")["price_twd_msq"].mean()

mean_price_by_age

house_age_years
0 to 15    12.64
15 to 30    9.88
30 to 45   11.39
Name: price_twd_msq, dtype: float64


Make a mental note of the results, as they’ll feature in the next exercise too.
Linear regression with a categorical explanatory variable
Great job calculating those grouped means! As mentioned, the means of each category will also be the coefficients of a linear regression model with one categorical variable. You’ll prove that in this exercise.
To run a linear regression model with categorical explanatory variables, you can use the same code as with numeric explanatory variables. The coefficients returned by the model are different, however. Here you’ll run a linear regression on the Taiwan real estate dataset.
Instructions:

Run and fit a linear regression with price_twd_msq as the response variable, house_age_years as the explanatory variable, and taiwan_real_estate as the dataset. Assign to mdl_price_vs_age.
Print its parameters.


# Create the model, fit it
mdl_price_vs_age = ols("price_twd_msq ~ house_age_years", data=taiwan_real_estate).fit()

mdl_price_vs_age.params

Intercept                     12.64
house_age_years[T.15 to 30]   -2.76
house_age_years[T.30 to 45]   -1.24
dtype: float64



Update the model formula so that no intercept is included in the model. Assign to mdl_price_vs_age0.
Print its parameters.


# Update the model formula to remove the intercept
mdl_price_vs_age0 = ols("price_twd_msq ~ house_age_years + 0", data=taiwan_real_estate).fit()

mdl_price_vs_age0.params

house_age_years[0 to 15]    12.64
house_age_years[15 to 30]    9.88
house_age_years[30 to 45]   11.39
dtype: float64


The coefficients of the model are just the means of each category you calculated previously.

title: Introduction to Regression with statsmodels in Python
text:
2 Predictions and model objects
In this chapter, you’ll discover how to use linear regression models to make predictions on Taiwanese house prices and Facebook advert clicks. You’ll also grow your regression skills as you get hands-on with model objects, understand the concept of “regression to the mean”, and learn how to transform variables in a dataset.

2.1 Making predictions
The big benefit of running models rather than simply calculating descriptive statistics is that models let you make predictions. Here’s the fish dataset again. This time, we’ll look only at the bream data. There’s a new explanatory variable too: the length of each fish, which we’ll use to predict the mass of the fish.

Here’s a scatter plot of mass versus length for the bream data, with a linear trend line.

Before we can make predictions, we need a fitted model. As before, we call ols() with a formula and the dataset, after which we add .fit(). The response, mass in grams, goes on the left-hand side of the formula, and the explanatory variable, length in centimeters, goes on the right. We need to assign the result to a variable to reuse later on. To view the coefficients of the model, we use the .params attribute in a print call.

The principle behind predicting is to ask questions of the form “if I set the explanatory variables to these values, what value would the response variable have?”. That means that the next step is to choose some values for the explanatory variables. To create new explanatory data, we need to store our explanatory variables of choice in a pandas DataFrame. You can use a dictionary to specify the columns. For this model, the only explanatory variable is the length of the fish. You can specify an interval of values using the np.arange() function, taking the start and end of the interval as arguments. Notice that the end of the interval does not include this value. Here, I specified a range of 20 to 40 centimeters.

The next step is to call .predict() on the model, passing the DataFrame of explanatory variables as the argument. The .predict() function returns a Series of predictions, one for each row of the explanatory data.

Having a single column of predictions isn’t that helpful to work with. It’s easier to work with if the predictions are in a DataFrame alongside the explanatory variables. To do this, you can use the pandas .assign() method. It returns a new object with all original columns in addition to new ones. You start with the existing column, explanatory_data. Then, you use .assign() to add a new column, named after the response variable, mass_g. You calculate it with the same .predict() code from brefore. The resulting DataFrame contains both the explanatory variable and the predicted response. Now we can answer questions like “how heavy would we expect a bream with length 23 centimeters to be?”, even though the original dataset didn’t include a bream of that exact length. Looking at the prediction data, you can see that the predicted mass is two 219.

Let’s include the predictions we just made on the scatter plot. To plot multiple layers, we set a matplotlib figure object called fig before calling regplot() and scatterplot(). As a result, the plt.show() call will then plot both graphs on the same figure. I’ve marked the prediction points in red squares to distinguish them from the actual data points. Notice that the predictions lie exactly on the trend line.

All the fish were between 23 and 38 centimeters, but the linear model allows us to make predictions outside that range. This is called extrapolating. Let’s see what prediction we get for a 10 centimeter bream. To achieve this, you first create a DataFrame with a single observation of 10 cm. You then predict the corresponding mass as before.

The predicted mass is almost -500 grams! This is obviously not physically possible, so the model performs poorly here. Extrapolation is sometimes appropriate, but can lead to misleading or ridiculous results. You need to understand the context of your data in order to determine whether it is sensible to extrapolate.
Predicting house prices
Perhaps the most useful feature of statistical models like linear regression is that you can make predictions. That is, you specify values for each of the explanatory variables, feed them to the model, and get a prediction for the corresponding response variable. The code flow is as follows.
explanatory_data = pd.DataFrame({"explanatory_var": list_of_values})
predictions = model.predict(explanatory_data)
prediction_data = explanatory_data.assign(response_var=predictions)
Here, you’ll make predictions for the house prices in the Taiwan real estate dataset. The fitted linear regression model of house price versus number of convenience stores is available as mdl_price_vs_conv.

mdl_price_vs_conv = ols("price_twd_msq ~ n_convenience", data=taiwan_real_estate).fit()

Instructions:

Create a DataFrame of explanatory data, where the number of convenience stores, n_convenience, takes the integer values from 0 to 10.
Print explanatory_data.


# Create the explanatory_data 
explanatory_data = pd.DataFrame({'n_convenience': np.arange(0, 11)})

explanatory_data

Use the model mdl_price_vs_conv to make predictions from explanatory_data and store it as price_twd_msq.
Print the predictions.


price_twd_msq = mdl_price_vs_conv.predict(explanatory_data)

price_twd_msq

Create a DataFrame of predictions named prediction_data. Start with explanatory_data, then add an extra column, price_twd_msq, containing the predictions you created in the previous step.


# Create prediction_data
prediction_data = explanatory_data.assign(
    price_twd_msq = price_twd_msq)

prediction_data

Visualizing predictions
The prediction DataFrame you created contains a column of explanatory variable values and a column of response variable values. That means you can plot it on the same scatter plot of response versus explanatory data values.
Instructions:

Create a new figure to plot multiple layers.
Extend the plotting code to add points for the predictions in prediction_data. Color the points red.
Display the layered plot.


# Create a new figure, fig
fig = plt.figure()

sns.regplot(x="n_convenience",
            y="price_twd_msq",
            data=taiwan_real_estate,
            ci=None)

# Add a scatter plot layer to the regplot
sns.scatterplot(x="n_convenience",
y="price_twd_msq",
data=prediction_data,
color="red")

plt.show()








Naturally, the predicted points lie on the trend line.
The limits of prediction
In the last exercise, you made predictions on some sensible, could-happen-in-real-life, situations. That is, the cases when the number of nearby convenience stores were between zero and ten. To test the limits of the model’s ability to predict, try some impossible situations.
Try predicting house prices from mdl_price_vs_conv when there are -1 convenience stores. Do the same for 2.5 convenience stores. What happens in each case?
Instructions: - Create some impossible explanatory data. Define a DataFrame impossible with one column, n_convenience, set to -1 in the first row, and 2.5 in the second row. - Try making predictions on your two impossible cases. What happens?

impossible = pd.DataFrame({"n_convenience": [-1, 2.5]})

mdl_price_vs_conv.predict(impossible)

0    7.43
1   10.22
dtype: float64


The model successfully gives a prediction about cases that are impossible in real life. Linear models don’t know what is possible or not in real life. That means that they can give you predictions that don’t make any sense when applied to your data. You need to understand what your data means in order to determine whether a prediction is nonsense or not.


2.2 Working with model objects
The model objects created by ols contain a lot of information. You’ll see how to extract it. You already learned how to extract the coefficients or parameters from your fitted model. You add the .params attribute, which will return a pandas Series including your intercept and slope.

Fitted values is jargon for predictions on the original dataset used to create the model. Access them with the .fittedvalues attribute. The result is a pandas Series of length 35, which is the number of rows in the bream dataset. The .fittedvalues attribute is essentially a shortcut for taking the explanatory variable columns from the dataset, then feeding them to the .predict() function.

The residuals are a measure of inaccuracy in the model fit, and are accessed with the .resid attribute. Like fitted values, there is one residual for each row of the dataset. Each residual is the actual response value minus the predicted response value. In this case, the residuals are the masses of breams, minus the fitted values. I illustrated the residuals as red lines on the regression plot. Each vertical line represents a single residual. You’ll see more on how to use the fitted values and residuals to assess the quality of your model in Chapter 3.

The .summary() method shows a more extended printout of the details of the model. Let’s step through this piece by piece.

First, you see the dependent variable(s) that were used in the model, in addition to the type of regression. You also see some metrics on the performance of the model. These will be discussed in the next chapter.

Extracting model elements
The model object created by ols() contains many elements. In order to perform further analysis on the model results, you need to extract its useful bits. The model coefficients, the fitted values, and the residuals are perhaps the most important pieces of the linear model object.
mdl_price_vs_conv is available.

mdl_price_vs_conv = ols("price_twd_msq ~ n_convenience", data=taiwan_real_estate).fit()

Instructions:

Print the parameters of mdl_price_vs_conv.
Print the fitted values of mdl_price_vs_conv.
Print the residuals of mdl_price_vs_conv.
Print a summary of mdl_price_vs_conv.


mdl_price_vs_conv.params

Intercept       8.22
n_convenience   0.80
dtype: float64



mdl_price_vs_conv.fittedvalues

Manually predicting house prices
You can manually calculate the predictions from the model coefficients. When making predictions in real life, it is better to use .predict(), but doing this manually is helpful to reassure yourself that predictions aren’t magic - they are simply arithmetic.
In fact, for a simple linear regression, the predicted value is just the intercept plus the slope times the explanatory variable.
\[\text{response} = \text{intercept} + \text{slope}\times \text{explanatory}\]
explanatory_data is available.

explanatory_data = pd.DataFrame({'n_convenience': np.arange(0, 11)})

Instructions:

Get the coefficients/parameters of mdl_price_vs_conv, assigning to coeffs.
Get the intercept, which is the first element of coeffs, assigning to intercept.
Get the slope, which is the second element of coeffs, assigning to slope.
Manually predict price_twd_msq using the formula, specifying the intercept, slope, and explanatory_data.
Run the code to compare your manually calculated predictions to the results from .predict().


# Get the coefficients of mdl_price_vs_conv
coeffs = mdl_price_vs_conv.params

# Get the intercept
intercept = coeffs.iloc[0]

# Get the slope
slope = coeffs.iloc[1]

# Manually calculate the predictions
price_twd_msq = intercept + slope * explanatory_data
price_twd_msq

price_twd_msq.assign(predictions_auto=mdl_price_vs_conv.predict(explanatory_data))

2.3 Regression to the mean
Let’s take a short break from thinking about regression modeling, to a related concept called “regression to the mean”. Regression to the mean is a property of the data, not a type of model, but linear regression can be used to quantify its effect.
You already saw that each response value in your dataset is equal to the sum of a fitted value, that is, the prediction by the model, and a residual, which is how much the model missed by. Loosely speaking, these two values are the parts of the response that you’ve explained why it has that value, and the parts you couldn’t explain with your model. There are two possibilities for why you have a residual. Firstly, it could just be because your model isn’t great. Particularly in the case of simple linear regression where you only have one explanatory variable, there is often room for improvement. However, it usually isn’t possible or desirable to have a perfect model because the world contains a lot of randomness, and your model shouldn’t capture that. In particular, extreme responses are often due to randomness or luck. That means that extremes don’t persist over time, because eventually the luck runs out. This is the concept of regression to the mean. Eventually, extreme cases will look more like average cases.
Here’s a classic dataset on the heights of fathers and their sons, collected by Karl Pearson, the statistician who the Pearson correlation coefficient is named after. The dataset consists of over a thousand pairs of heights, and was collected as part of a nineteenth century scientific work on biological inheritance. It lets us answer the question, “do tall fathers have tall sons?”, and “do short fathers have short sons?”.

Here’s a scatter plot of the sons’ heights versus the fathers’ heights. I’ve added a line where the son’s and father’s heights are equal, using plt.axline(). The first two arguments determine the intercept and slope, while the linewidth and color arguments help it stand out. I also used plt.axis() with the "equal" argument so that one centimeter on the x-axis appears the same as one centimeter on the y-axis. If sons always had the same height as their fathers, all the points would lie on this green line.

Let’s add a black linear regression line to the plot using regplot(). You can see that the regression line isn’t as steep as the first line. On the left of the plot, the black line is above the green line, suggesting that for very short fathers, their sons are taller than them on average. On the far right of the plot, the black line is below the green line, suggesting that for very tall fathers, their sons are shorter than them on average.

Running a model quantifies the predictions of how much taller or shorter the sons will be. Here, the sons’ heights are the response variable, and the fathers’ heights are the explanatory variable.

Now we can make predictions. Consider the case of a really tall father, at 190 centimeters. At least, that was really tall in the late nineteenth century. The predicted height of the son is 183 centimeters. Tall, but not quite as tall as his dad. Similarly, the prediction for a 150 centimeter father is 163 centimeters. Short, but not quite as short as his dad. In both cases, the extreme value became less extreme in the next generation — a perfect example of regression to the mean.

Home run!
Regression to the mean is an important concept in many areas, including sports.
Here you can see a dataset of baseball batting data in 2017 and 2018. Each point represents a player, and more home runs are better. A naive prediction might be that the performance in 2018 is the same as the performance in 2017. That is, a linear regression would lie on the “y equals x” line.
Explore the plot and make predictions. What does regression to the mean say about the number of home runs in 2018 for a player who was very successful in 2017?

Someone who hit 40 home runs in 2017 is predicted to hit 10 fewer home runs the next year because regression to the mean states that, on average, extremely high values are not sustained.
Plotting consecutive portfolio returns
Regression to the mean is also an important concept in investing. Here you’ll look at the annual returns from investing in companies in the Standard and Poor 500 index (S&P 500), in 2018 and 2019.
The sp500_yearly_returns dataset contains three columns:



variable
meaning




symbol
Stock ticker symbol uniquely identifying the company.


return_2018
A measure of investment performance in 2018.


return_2019
A measure of investment performance in 2019.



A positive number for the return means the investment increased in value; negative means it lost value. Just as with baseball home runs, a naive prediction might be that the investment performance stays the same from year to year, lying on the y equals x line.

sp500_yearly_returns = pd.read_csv("data26/sp500_yearly_returns.csv")

itables.show(sp500_yearly_returns)

Instructions:

Create a new figure, fig, to enable plot layering.
Generate a line at y equals x.
Using sp500_yearly_returns, draw a scatter plot of return_2019 vs. return_2018 with a linear regression trend line, without a standard error ribbon.
Set the axes so that the distances along the x and y axes look the same.


# Create a new figure, fig
fig = plt.figure()

The regression trend line looks very different to the y equals x line. As the financial advisors say, “Past performance is no guarantee of future results.”
Modeling consecutive returns
Let’s quantify the relationship between returns in 2019 and 2018 by running a linear regression and making predictions. By looking at companies with extremely high or extremely low returns in 2018, we can see if their performance was similar in 2019.
Instructions:

Run a linear regression on return_2019 versus return_2018 using sp500_yearly_returns and fit the model. Assign to mdl_returns.
Print the parameters of the model.


mdl_returns = ols("return_2019 ~ return_2018", data=sp500_yearly_returns).fit()

mdl_returns.params

Intercept     0.32
return_2018   0.02
dtype: float64



Create a DataFrame named explanatory_data. Give it one column (return_2018) with 2018 returns set to a list containing -1, 0, and 1.
Use mdl_returns to predict with explanatory_data.


explanatory_data = pd.DataFrame({"return_2018": [-1, 0, 1]})

mdl_returns.predict(explanatory_data)

0   0.30
1   0.32
2   0.34
dtype: float64


Investments that gained a lot in value in 2018 on average gained only a small amount in 2019. Similarly, investments that lost a lot of value in 2018 on average also gained a small amount in 2019.


2.4 Transforming variables
Sometimes, the relationship between the explanatory variable and the response variable may not be a straight line. To fit a linear regression model, you may need to transform the explanatory variable or the response variable, or both of them.
Consider the perch in the fish dataset.

The upward curve in the mass versus length data prevents us drawing a straight line that follows it closely.

To understand why the bream had a strong linear relationship between mass and length, but the perch didn’t, you need to understand your data. I’m not a fish expert, but looking at the picture of the bream on the left, it has a very narrow body. I guess that as bream get bigger, they mostly get longer and not wider. By contrast, the perch on the right has a round body, so I guess that as it grows, it gets fatter and taller as well as longer. Since the perches are growing in three directions at once, maybe the length cubed will give a better fit.

Here’s an update to the previous plot. The only change is that the x-axis is now length to the power of three. To do this, first create an additional column where you calculate the length cubed. Then replace this newly created column in your regplot() call. The data points fit the line much better now, so we’re ready to run a model.

To model this transformation, we replace the original length variable with the cubed length variable. We then fit the model and extract its coefficients.

We create the explanatory DataFrame in the same way as usual. Notice that you specify the lengths cubed. We can also add the untransformed lengths column for reference. The code for adding predictions is the same .assign() and .predict() combination as you’ve seen before.

The predictions have been added to the plot of mass versus length cubed as red points. As you might expect, they follow the line drawn by regplot(). It gets more interesting on the original x-axis. Notice how the red points curve upwards to follow the data. Your linear model has non-linear predictions, after the transformation is undone.

Let’s try one more example using a Facebook advertising dataset. The flow of online advertising is that you pay money to Facebook, who show your advert to Facebook users. If a person sees the advert, it’s called an impression. Then some people who see the advert will click on it.

Let’s look at impressions versus spend. If we draw the standard plot, the majority of the points are crammed into the bottom-left of the plot, making it difficult to assess whether there is a good fit or not.

By transforming both the variables with square roots, the data are more spread out throughout the plot, and the points follow the line fairly closely. Square roots are a common transformation when your data has a right-skewed distribution.

Running the model and creating the explanatory dataset are the same as usual, but notice the use of the transformed variables in the formula and DataFrame. I also included the untransformed spent_usd variable for reference. Prediction requires an extra step. Because we took the square root of the response variable (not just the explanatory variable), the .predict() function will predict the square root of the number of impressions. That means that we have to undo the square root by squaring the predicted responses. Undoing the transformation of the response is called back transformation.

Transforming the explanatory variable
If there is no straight-line relationship between the response variable and the explanatory variable, it is sometimes possible to create one by transforming one or both of the variables. Here, you’ll look at transforming the explanatory variable.
You’ll take another look at the Taiwan real estate dataset, this time using the distance to the nearest MRT (metro) station as the explanatory variable. You’ll use code to make every commuter’s dream come true: shortening the distance to the metro station by taking the square root. Take that, geography!
Instructions:

Look at the plot.


sns.regplot(
    data=taiwan_real_estate,
    x="dist_to_mrt_m",
    y="price_twd_msq",
    ci=None
)

plt.show()

Transforming the response variable too
The response variable can be transformed too, but this means you need an extra step at the end to undo that transformation. That is, you “back transform” the predictions.
In the video, you saw the first step of the digital advertising workflow: spending money to buy ads, and counting how many people see them (the “impressions”). The next step is determining how many people click on the advert after seeing it.

title: Introduction to Regression with statsmodels in Python
text:
3 Assessing model fit
In this chapter, you’ll learn how to ask questions of your model to assess fit. You’ll learn how to quantify how well a linear regression model fits, diagnose model problems using visualizations, and understand each observation’s leverage and influence to create the model.

3.1 Quantifying model fit
It’s usually essential to know whether or not predictions from your model are nonsense. In this chapter, we’ll look at ways of quantifying how good your model is.
Previously, you ran models on mass versus length for bream and perch. By merely looking at these scatter plots, you can get a sense that there is a linear relationship between mass and length for bream but not for perch. It would be useful to quantify how strong that linear relationship is.

Coefficient of determination (r-squared or R-squared)
The first metric we’ll discuss is the coefficient of determination. This is sometimes called “r-squared”. For boring historical reasons, it’s written with a lower case r for simple linear regression and an upper case R when you have more than one explanatory variable. It is defined as the proportion of the variance in the response variable that is predictable from the explanatory variable. We’ll get to a human-readable explanation shortly. A score of 1 means you have a perfect fit, and a score of 0 means your model is no better than randomness. What constitutes a good score depends on your dataset. A score of 0.5 on a psychological experiment may be exceptionally high because humans are inherently hard to predict, but in other cases, a score of 0.9 may be considered a poor fit.
The .summary() method shows several performance metrics in its output. The coefficient of determination is written in the first line and titled R-squared. Its value is about 0.88.

Since the output of .summary() isn’t easy to work with, a better way to extract the metric is to use the .rsquared attribute, which contains the r-squared value as a float.

For simple linear regression, the interpretation of the coefficient of determination is straightforward. It is simply the correlation between the explanatory and response variables, squared.

Residual standard error (RSE)
The second metric we’ll look at is the residual standard error, or RSE. Recall that each residual is the difference between a predicted value and an observed value. The RSE is, very roughly speaking, a measure of the typical size of the residuals. That is, how much the predictions are typically wrong. It has the same unit as the response variable. In the fish models, the response unit is grams. A related, but less commonly used metric is the mean squared error (MSE). As the name suggests, MSE is the squared residual standard error (\(\text{MSE} = \text{RSE}^2\)).

The .summary() method unfortunately doesn’t contain the RSE. However, it can indirectly be retrieved from the .mse_resid attribute, which contains the mean squared error of the residuals. We can calculate the RSE by taking the square root of MSE. As such, the RSE has the same unit as the response variable. The RSE for the bream model is about 74.

To calculate the RSE yourself, it’s slightly more complicated. First, you take the square of each residual. Then you take the sum of these residuals squared. You then calculate the degrees of freedom of the residuals. This is the number of observations \(n\) minus the number of model coefficients, in this case \(2\). Finally, you take the square root of the ratio of those two numbers.
\[\text{RSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-2} \sum_{i=1}^n \left( y_i - \widehat{y}_i\right)^2}\]
Reassuringly, the value is still 74.

An RSE of 74 means that the difference between predicted bream masses and observed bream masses is typically about 74 grams.
**_ Root-mean-square error (RMSE)_**
Another related metric is the root-mean-square error. This is calculated in the same way, except you don’t subtract the number of coefficients in the second to last step:
\[\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left( y_i - \widehat{y}_i\right)^2}\]
It performs the same task as residual standard error, namely quantifying how inaccurate the model predictions are, but is worse for comparisons between models. You need to be aware that RMSE exists, but typically you should use RSE instead.
Coefficient of determination
The coefficient of determination is a measure of how well the linear regression line fits the observed values. For simple linear regression, it is equal to the square of the correlation between the explanatory and response variables.
Here, you’ll take another look at the second stage of the advertising pipeline: modeling the click response to impressions. Two models are available: mdl_click_vs_impression_orig models n_clicks versus n_impressions. mdl_click_vs_impression_trans is the transformed model you saw in Chapter 2. It models n_clicks to the power of 0.25 versus n_impressions to the power of 0.25.

mdl_click_vs_impression_orig = ols("n_clicks ~ n_impressions", data=ad_conversion).fit()

ad_conversion["qdrt_n_impressions"] = ad_conversion["n_impressions"] ** 0.25
ad_conversion["qdrt_n_clicks"] = ad_conversion["n_clicks"] ** 0.25
mdl_click_vs_impression_trans = ols("qdrt_n_clicks ~ qdrt_n_impressions", data=ad_conversion).fit()

Instructions:

Print the summary of mdl_click_vs_impression_orig.
Do the same for mdl_click_vs_impression_trans.


print(mdl_click_vs_impression_orig.summary())
